ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no concept for Singapore hawker Western chicken chop. Closest parent is a fried chicken with sides (FOODON:03315595 — fried chicken food product). No exact URI available; null used with honest gap note.
ontology.foodex2_description: Poultry meat, chicken — used as closest-parent FoodEx2 anchor for Singapore Western hawker chicken chop with fries and coleslaw. Match type: closest_parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0BDF covers chicken meat broadly; no specific code exists for the Singapore hawker Western chicken chop composite dish with fries, mushroom sauce, and coleslaw.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Singapore hawker Western chicken chop composite dish.

main_ingredients: mushroom cream sauce (button mushrooms, cream, seasoning)
allergens: dairy (cream in mushroom sauce, coleslaw mayonnaise)

serving.natural_language_portion: One full plate of Singapore hawker Western chicken chop weighing approximately 620g, comprising 200g fried chicken thigh, 200g thick-cut fries, 80g mushroom cream sauce, 80g coleslaw, and 60g bread roll.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Chop (Western Hawker) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Western hawker chicken chop used as primary nutrient source. Values reflect the composite dish as served at Singapore kopitiam Western stalls including chicken, fries, mushroom sauce, coleslaw, and bread. All per-100g values sourced directly from this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of French Fries and Fried Chicken Composite Meals — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed analogous estimates
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Singapore hawker Western chicken chop as a composite dish. GI of 70 is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entries for deep-fried potato chips/french fries (GI ~63–75, mean ~70) and flour-coated fried chicken (GI contribution from coating). The fries (~200g, ~32g carbohydrate) and flour coating (~15g carbohydrate) dominate the glycaemic load. Glycemic load per 620g serving calculated as: (GI 70 × 98g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 68.6. This is a high-GL meal. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium — 388mg per 100g; a full 620g serving delivers approximately 2,405mg sodium, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000mg from a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or significantly limit chicken chop; if consuming, request mushroom sauce on the side and reduced fries, as the sauce and salted fries are the primary sodium carriers, and pair with unseasoned vegetables to partially offset sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load per serving (GL 68.6) driven by approximately 98g available carbohydrate at GI 70 from thick-cut fries, flour coating, and bread roll
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should strongly limit or avoid chicken chop; if consuming, request fries replaced with salad or non-starchy vegetables, skip the bread roll, eat the chicken protein first, and request sauce on the side to reduce both carbohydrate load and sodium.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (2.8g/100g; ~17.4g per serving), trans fat (0.1g/100g; ~0.6g per serving from frying oil degradation), very high sodium (2,405mg per serving), and high cholesterol (45mg/100g; ~279mg per serving) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit chicken chop due to the combination of high saturated fat, trans fat, sodium, and cholesterol; if consuming occasionally, remove the fried skin and coating, skip the bread, request sauce on the side, and substitute fries with a green salad.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High glycaemic load drives glycaemic-inflammatory spikes; trans fat and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) from deep-frying the flour coating promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit chicken chop; if consuming, remove the fried coating, substitute fries with salad, and request less sauce to reduce AGE, trans fat, and glycaemic inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Chop with Fries is the flagship dish of Singapore's hawker Western stalls — a uniquely Singaporean adaptation of European pork and chicken chop traditions, developed by Hainanese cooks who learned Western cooking techniques from colonial employers and later opened their own kopitiam stalls. A large chicken thigh — bone-in or boned, always skin-on — is marinated in a savoury blend, lightly coated in seasoned flour, and deep-fried until golden-brown and crispy on the outside while remaining juicy inside. It is plated with a generous heap of thick-cut deep-fried potato fries, a small scoop of creamy coleslaw (shredded cabbage and carrot in mayonnaise dressing), and a ladleful of rich mushroom cream sauce (cream of mushroom with sliced button mushrooms) poured generously over the chicken and fries. A dinner roll or thick white toast typically accompanies the plate. The Singapore hawker Western version is distinguished from its European counterparts by its generous portion size (a full plate weighs approximately 620g and delivers 1,100 kcal), the sweet-savoury mushroom cream sauce that is slightly thicker and sweeter than European versions, and the accompanying white bread. Nutritionally, a full chicken chop plate is one of the most calorie- and carbohydrate-dense hawker meals in Singapore, delivering approximately 98g carbohydrate, 65g fat, and 2,405mg sodium per serving. The glycaemic load per serving is very high at 68.6, driven primarily by the thick-cut fries, flour coating, and bread roll. The dish is high in protein (65g per serving) from the chicken thigh, and provides meaningful amounts of niacin, vitamin B6, phosphorus, and potassium. It is not suitable for patients with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The dish is widely available at Singapore kopitiams and hawker centres and is considered a comfort food staple across all ethnic communities in Singapore.
common_user_queries: is chicken chop good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient data sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the authoritative approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for deep-fried potato fries and flour-coated fried chicken with confidence 0.9. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from the verified HPB source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Singapore hawker Western chicken chop dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. All other nutrient fields are fully populated from the HPB verified source. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for deep-fried french fries (GI ~70) and flour-coated fried chicken; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Glycemic load per 620g serving = (70 × 98g carbohydrate) / 100 = 68.6. The dish is a Singaporean adaptation of Western chicken chop traditions and is not found in Western food composition databases; HPB Foodo is the sole approved primary source for this record.
